title: NOC 94133 – Inspectors and graders, textile, fabric, fur and leather products manufacturing | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s textile and leather industries have moved toward "Smart Quality Assurance." Inspectors and Graders (NOC 94133) are the technical gatekeepers who ensure that high-performance fabrics, aerospace-grade leathers, and luxury fur products meet rigorous global standards. As manufacturing becomes more automated, these professionals are increasingly utilizing digital scanning and AI-assisted defect detection to maintain Canada's reputation for premium quality. With over 10,000 job openings projected through the next decade, this role offers a stable pathway for detail-oriented international workers.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Occupations usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Machine operators in mineral and metal processing

duties: Conformity Examination: Examining natural, synthetic, and hybrid textile products to ensure they meet engineering or design quality standards.
Grading: Inspecting and grading animal hides and pelts according to size, condition, weight, and color consistency.
Measurement Verification: Verifying the fit or size of finished garments by comparing them against digital patterns or physical samples.
Defect Correction: Marking defects and repairing minor imperfections, such as removing lint or spots using specialized cleaning solutions.
Reporting: Recording detailed inspection data for quality management systems and tagging products for distribution.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education is usually required; a high school diploma is preferred for 2026 technical roles.
Experience: Previous work experience in the manufacturing of the specific products being inspected is typically required.
Training: Several weeks of specialized on-the-job training are provided by the employer.
Regulation: This occupation is not regulated in Canada, meaning no mandatory license is required to start working.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) projects 10,900 new job openings against 10,200 job seekers, indicating a slight labor shortage. With nearly 70% of openings resulting from retirements, the sector is heavily reliant on international talent to maintain its quality control workforce.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by the demand for "Made in Canada" quality and high-tech textile applications:
Quebec (Montreal): High demand. As Canada's fashion and textile hub, Montreal has a chronic shortage of graders for luxury leather and fur products.
Ontario (GTA): Very Good prospects. Driven by the automotive and aerospace industries requiring high-precision inspection of interior fabrics and leathers.
British Columbia: Stable demand. Focused on specialized outdoor apparel and technical textile inspection in the Lower Mainland.
Manitoba (Winnipeg): Good prospects. Winnipeg’s robust garment manufacturing sector maintains a steady need for quality control specialists.
Why Work in Canada as an Inspector or Grader?
Technical Quality Focus: Transition from manual checks to operating 2026-grade laser surface scanners and digital colorimetry tools.
Diverse Industry Impact: Your work spans sectors from luxury fashion and furniture to safety-critical automotive airbags and medical textiles.
Path to PR: Frequently prioritized in Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) semi-skilled streams and the 2026 TR to PR transition programs.
High Level of Benefits: 100% of workers in this NOC nationwide receive employer-sponsored benefits, including dental, medical, and life insurance.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): Inspectors and graders are a high-priority "Essential Manufacturing" occupation for this 33,000-person transition stream.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario’s industrial sectors.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A high-success pathway for those working in regional manufacturing hubs where textile or leather processing is a primary employer.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled workers to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
